title: Bursa

infobox.value: *Bursa and [Cumalıkızık](/wiki/Cumal%C4%B1k%C4%B1z%C4%B1k "Cumalıkızık"): the Birth of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")*
[Europe](/wiki/Lists_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Europe "Lists of World Heritage Sites in Europe")

or **Prousa** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Προῦσα), or **Prusa near Olympus** or **Prusa under Olympus** (Προῦσα ἐπὶ τῷ Ὀλύμπῳ, Προῦσα πρὸς τῷ Ὀλύμπῳ). The city became the capital of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") (back then the Ottoman Beylik) from 1335 until the 1360s.

after its location at the foot of the Bithynian Olympus (present day [Mount Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ")).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthon18511135-10) One of the known characteristics of Prusa at that time was its hot springs that's dubbed as the "royal waters".[[9]](#cite_note-11) In 75/74 BC, [Nicomedes IV](/wiki/Nicomedes_IV_of_Bithynia "Nicomedes IV of Bithynia"), the last king of [Bithynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bithynia "Kingdom of Bithynia"), bequeated his entire kingdom to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in his last testament before he died.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMayor2010260-12)

**Bursa**[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a city in northwestern [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and the administrative center of [Bursa Province](/wiki/Bursa_Province "Bursa Province"). It is the [fourth-most populous](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Turkey "List of cities in Turkey") city in Turkey and second-most populous in the [Marmara Region](/wiki/Marmara_Region "Marmara Region") after [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"). According to 2025 end of year estimate, the province has a population of 3,263,011 while Bursa city has a population of around 2.5 million.[[b]](#cite_note-4) Bursa is one of the centers of [Turkey's automotive production](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Turkey "Automotive industry in Turkey"), becoming an industrial center of the country.[[3]](#cite_note-5) The city provides various [places of interest](/wiki/Places_of_interest_in_Bursa "Places of interest in Bursa").

Historically, Bursa was known as **Prusa** or **Prousa** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Προῦσα), or **Prusa near Olympus** or **Prusa under Olympus** (Προῦσα ἐπὶ τῷ Ὀλύμπῳ, Προῦσα πρὸς τῷ Ὀλύμπῳ). The city became the capital of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") (back then the Ottoman Beylik) from 1335 until the 1360s.

A more recent nickname is *Yeşil Bursa* ("Green Bursa") referring to the parks and gardens located across the city, as well as to the vast, varied forests of the surrounding region. Bursa has a rather orderly urban growth and borders a fertile plain. The [mausoleums](/wiki/Mausoleums "Mausoleums") of the early [Ottoman sultans](/wiki/Ottoman_Dynasty "Ottoman Dynasty") are located in Bursa, and the city's main landmarks include numerous edifices built throughout the Ottoman period. Bursa also has [thermal baths](/wiki/Thermal_bath "Thermal bath"), old Ottoman mansions, palaces, and several museums. [Mount Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ"), known in [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") as the [Mysian Olympus](/wiki/Mysian_Olympus "Mysian Olympus") or, alternatively, [Bithynian Olympus](/wiki/Bithynian_Olympus "Bithynian Olympus"), towers over the city and has a ski resort.

The [shadow play](/wiki/Shadow_play "Shadow play") characters [Karagöz and Hacivat](/wiki/Karag%C3%B6z_and_Hacivat "Karagöz and Hacivat"), according to some stories, are based on historic personalities who lived and died in Bursa in the 14th century.[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Timeline of Bursa](/wiki/Timeline_of_Bursa "Timeline of Bursa") and [Prousa](/wiki/Prousa "Prousa")

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Archaeological_Museum_6978.jpg/250px-Archaeological_Museum_6978.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archaeological_Museum_6978.jpg)

[Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena"), bronze, 2nd century AD, at [Bursa Archaeological Museum](/wiki/Bursa_Archaeological_Museum "Bursa Archaeological Museum")

The earliest known human settlement near Bursa's current location was at the [Ilıpınar Mound](/wiki/Il%C4%B1p%C4%B1nar_Mound "Ilıpınar Mound") in c. 6000 BC.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoodenbergAlpaslan-Roodenberg201369-8) It was followed by the Bithynian city of [Prusa](/wiki/Prusa_(Bithynia) "Prusa (Bithynia)"), which was built by [King](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Bithynia "List of rulers of Bithynia") [Prusias I of Bithynia](/wiki/Prusias_I_of_Bithynia "Prusias I of Bithynia").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowie202274-9) The city was also referred to as **Prusa ad Olympum** after its location at the foot of the Bithynian Olympus (present day [Mount Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ")).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthon18511135-10) One of the known characteristics of Prusa at that time was its hot springs that's dubbed as the "royal waters".[[9]](#cite_note-11) In 75/74 BC, [Nicomedes IV](/wiki/Nicomedes_IV_of_Bithynia "Nicomedes IV of Bithynia"), the last king of [Bithynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bithynia "Kingdom of Bithynia"), bequeated his entire kingdom to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in his last testament before he died.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMayor2010260-12)

According to a letter that's written to [Roman Emperor](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor") [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") (r. 98–117) by Roman author [Pliny the Younger](/wiki/Pliny_the_Younger "Pliny the Younger"), then the [Imperial Governor](/wiki/Roman_governor "Roman governor") of [Bithynia and Pontus](/wiki/Bithynia_and_Pontus "Bithynia and Pontus"), constructions of [baths](/wiki/Thermae "Thermae") took place in Prusa after a permit by the reigning emperor.

> To Trajan.
>
> When I was looking about, Sir, for a place upon which to build the baths which you have graciously allowed to be erected at Prusa, I was pleased with a site on which there once stood, I am told, a beautiful mansion which is now in a ruinous and unsightly condition.

— [Epistulae](/wiki/Epistulae_(Pliny) "Epistulae (Pliny)"), book X.70[[11]](#cite_note-13)

[An early Roman artifact](/wiki/Bursa_Treasure "Bursa Treasure") was found in Bursa. It was composed of woman's silver toiletry articles. It is currently reserved in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") since 1913.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica") suggests that, when Prusa was under [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") rule, the city prospered after the [Byzantine Emperor](/wiki/List_of_Byzantine_emperors "List of Byzantine emperors") [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") built a palace there.[[13]](#cite_note-15) Prusa then became a garrison city in 562, where imperial guards were stationed. Already by the mid-6th century, Prusa was known as a famous silk textile manufacturing centre.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumperStanley2007101-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Bursa018.jpg/330px-Bursa018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bursa018.jpg)

[Ottoman architecture](/wiki/Ottoman_architecture "Ottoman architecture") in Bursa

Bursa became the capital city of the early Ottoman Empire following [its capture from the Byzantines in 1326](/wiki/Siege_of_Bursa "Siege of Bursa"). During the Ottoman rule, the city witnessed a considerable amount of urban growth, such as the building of hospitals, [caravanserais](/wiki/Caravanserai "Caravanserai") (including the [Koza Han](/wiki/Koza_Han "Koza Han")), and [madrasas](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumperStanley2007101-16) The first official Ottoman mint was established in the city.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumperStanley2007101-16) After [conquering](/wiki/Ottoman_conquest_of_Adrianople "Ottoman conquest of Adrianople") [Adrianople](/wiki/Orestias "Orestias") (later [Edirne](/wiki/Edirne "Edirne")) in [East Thrace](/wiki/East_Thrace "East Thrace"), the Ottomans turned it into the new capital city in the 1360s.[[c]](#cite_note-18) No longer a capital city, Bursa still retained its spiritual and commercial importance in the Ottoman Empire.[[16]](#cite_note-19) In Bursa, the [Ottoman Sultan](/wiki/List_of_sultans_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "List of sultans of the Ottoman Empire") [Bayezid I](/wiki/Bayezid_I "Bayezid I") built the Bayezid I Complex (which contains the [Bayezid I Mosque](/wiki/Bayezid_I_Mosque "Bayezid I Mosque")) between 1390 and 1395 and the [Grand Mosque of Bursa](/wiki/Grand_Mosque_of_Bursa "Grand Mosque of Bursa") between 1396 and 1400.[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21) After the defeat and capture of Bayezid in the [Battle of Ankara](/wiki/Battle_of_Ankara "Battle of Ankara") by the forces of [Emir](/wiki/Timurid_Dynasty#List_of_rulers "Timurid Dynasty") [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur") in 1402, the latter's grandson, [Muhammad Sultan Mirza](/wiki/Muhammad_Sultan_Mirza "Muhammad Sultan Mirza"), had Bursa pillaged and burned.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENizamiHabib1970128-22) Timur then assigned the administration of Bursa to his protégé, a son of [Savcı Bey](/wiki/Savc%C4%B1_Bey "Savcı Bey").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKastritsis200779-23) Bursa was later put under the control of Ottoman co-ruler and pretender [Îsâ Çelebi](/wiki/%C4%B0sa_%C3%87elebi "İsa Çelebi") during the [Ottoman interregnum](/wiki/Ottoman_Interregnum "Ottoman Interregnum") following the death of Bayezid in captivity.[[d]](#cite_note-24) On May 1403, Bursa was annexed by [Mehmed Çelebi](/wiki/Mehmed_I "Mehmed I") in the aftermath of the [Battle of Ulubad](/wiki/Battle_of_Ulubad "Battle of Ulubad").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKastritsis200779-23) According to a folio, Bursa is recorded to have a total of 174 [quarters](/wiki/Quarter_(urban_subdivision) "Quarter (urban subdivision)") which was inhabitated by 6,457 tax-paying heads of households in 1487.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaroqhi2008361–362-25)

Bursa was a hub of the Ottoman silk trade. The city housed a [dockyard](/wiki/Shipyard "Shipyard") for many cargo ships and became a place of distribution of silk and other commodities from the East, particularly [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), to the rest of the [Mediterranean world](/wiki/Mediterranean_basin "Mediterranean basin"), which included the [Italian city-states](/wiki/Italian_city-states "Italian city-states"), particularly [Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") and [Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen2021443-27)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELowry20039–10-28) Bursa was a part of the land route of the Armenian trade networks.[[25]](#cite_note-29) Bursa also became a resort town with many springs, centered in an area named Cekirge, such as the Ottoman [hammams](/wiki/Hammam "Hammam") Eski and Yeni Kaplıcas.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumperStanley2007101-16) Sometime during a [Devshirme](/wiki/Devshirme "Devshirme") levy in 1603-4, the villagers of Eğerciler (currently called [Eğerce](/wiki/E%C4%9Ferce,_Mudanya "Eğerce, Mudanya")), a Christian village in Bursa and provider of sheep to Istanbul, declared that the children of the village were very much needed as shepherds. They also asserted that even though they were not obliged to give any children to the army, the officers took some anyway. The Ottoman government responded by issuing a decree that commanded the return of the children.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYilmaz2015909-30) In 1827, bursa was set as the capital of [Hüdavendigâr Eyalet](/wiki/H%C3%BCdavendig%C3%A2r_Eyalet "Hüdavendigâr Eyalet") until, following the [Vilayet Law](/wiki/Vilayet_Law "Vilayet Law"), the [Hüdavendigâr Vilayet](/wiki/H%C3%BCdavendig%C3%A2r_vilayet "Hüdavendigâr vilayet") from 1867 to the [dissolution of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Dissolution of the Ottoman Empire") in 1922.

### Modern Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern Era")]

In July 1915, thousands of the Orthodox Christian populations took refugee in Bursa under the order of the Ottoman government under the political party [Committee for Union and Progress](/wiki/Committee_of_Union_and_Progress "Committee of Union and Progress") (CUP) after being forced out of their coastal villages. This mass-migration worsened the conditions of the Greek population of Bursa, who have previously managed to survive the attacks and boycotts of 1914. Deportation orders later came to the Armenian population of Bursa after a series of deportations in [Adapazari](/wiki/Adapazar%C4%B1 "Adapazarı"). Protestant population of Armenian descent in Bursa were initially spared from deportation. Killings even occurred in Çengiler, a village outside of Bursa, after some villagers tried to resist deportation.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGingeras2016171-31) During the [Greco-Turkish War](/wiki/Greco-Turkish_War_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Greco-Turkish War (1919–1922)"), the Greek [troops of Asia Minor](/wiki/Army_of_Asia_Minor "Army of Asia Minor") captured Bursa in the early July of 1920 after clashes with Turkish forces around the city.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumperStanley2007103-32) The Cretan artilleryman Vasilios Moustakis describes the event with the following words: "The Infantry had come through and set fire to the station. We saw an English general on horseback, who ordered the fire to be put out because if Bursa were burned, it would be harming Greece".[[29]](#cite_note-33)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] On 11 September 1922, the [Turkish Nationalist Army](/wiki/Turkish_Land_Forces "Turkish Land Forces") recaptured Bursa during the [Great Offensive](/wiki/Great_Offensive "Great Offensive").[[30]](#cite_note-34)

Bursa's industry, which was based off of foreign-owned silk factories since the 19th century, was accelerated and improved by the production of other industrial sectors, such as textile production, automotive manufacturing, and agriculture-based industries.[[31]](#cite_note-35) Public factories and private industrial enterprises are also enstablished in the city later during the Turkish Republic.[[32]](#cite_note-36)

[![Entrance to the building of the Bursa Chamber of Commerce and Industry (BTSO), a Bursa-based professional association that focuses on commerce and industry since 6 June 1889.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Bursa_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry_Entrance.jpg/250px-Bursa_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry_Entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bursa_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry_Entrance.jpg "Entrance to the building of the Bursa Chamber of Commerce and Industry (BTSO), a Bursa-based professional association that focuses on commerce and industry since 6 June 1889.")

Entrance to the building of the [Bursa Chamber of Commerce and Industry](/wiki/Bursa_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry "Bursa Chamber of Commerce and Industry") (BTSO), a Bursa-based [professional association](/wiki/Professional_association "Professional association") that focuses on commerce and industry since 6 June 1889.

The city has traditionally been a pole of attraction, and was a major centre for refugees from various ethnic backgrounds who immigrated to [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") from the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") during the loss of the [Ottoman territories in Europe](/wiki/Ottoman_territories_in_Europe "Ottoman territories in Europe") between the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The most recent arrival of [Balkan Turks](/wiki/Turks_in_Europe#Balkan_Turks "Turks in Europe") took place between the 1940s and 1990s, when the [People's Republic of Bulgaria](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Bulgaria "People's Republic of Bulgaria") expelled approximately 150,000 [Bulgarian Turks](/wiki/Bulgarian_Turks_in_Turkey "Bulgarian Turks in Turkey") to Turkey.[[33]](#cite_note-37) About one-third of these 150,000 Bulgarian Turkish refugees eventually settled in Bursa (especially in the Hürriyet neighbourhood). With the construction of new industrial zones in the period between 1980 and 2000, many people from the [eastern provinces of Turkey](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolia_Region "Eastern Anatolia Region") came and settled in Bursa.

### Jewish community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jewish community")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Bursa, initially home to a small [Romaniote Jewish](/wiki/Romaniote_Jews "Romaniote Jews") community, underwent a demographic shift with the arrival of [Sephardic Jews](/wiki/Sephardic_Jews "Sephardic Jews") who were [expelled from the Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Jews_from_Spain "Expulsion of Jews from Spain") in the late 15th century. The Sephardic majority quickly absorbed the Romaniotes, leading to a cultural and numerical dominance. [Judaeo-Spanish](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") became the daily language, and the community paid its [poll tax](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya") through the representative, the *kahya*.[[34]](#cite_note-:03222-38)

Throughout the Ottoman period, most Jews resided in Kuruçeşme, Bursa's Jewish quarter, home to three [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue"). Etz Chaim (Eṣ Ḥayyim), the oldest, predated the Ottoman conquest, while the Gerush and [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_Synagogue_(Bursa) "Mayor Synagogue (Bursa)") synagogues were established by Sephardic newcomers. Despite the 1851 fire destroying Etz Chaim, the other two remain, along with the Berut synagogue. Bursa also had a Jewish cemetery until recently.[[34]](#cite_note-:03222-38)

Though never a major center, Bursa's Jewish population fluctuated. Dubious data suggests 683 families in 1571/72, dropping to 141 by 1696/97. By 1883, there were 2,179 Jews, with an influx of 400 from [Akkerman](/wiki/Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi "Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi") in 1887. Pre-[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the population reached 3,500, but emigration reduced it to 140 by the early 21st century.[[34]](#cite_note-:03222-38)

Engaged in the local economy, Bursa's Jews were shop owners and involved in guilds. In the 16th and 17th centuries, they excelled in textile manufacturing, silk trade, [goldsmithing](/wiki/Goldsmith "Goldsmith"), and finance. Despite economic struggles in the 18th and 19th centuries, a 1886 report highlighted poverty.[[34]](#cite_note-:03222-38)

Bursa faced blood libels in 1592 and 1865. Despite its size, the community produced renowned [halakhic](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha") scholars across centuries. Modern schooling arrived in 1886 with [Alliance Israélite Universelle](/wiki/Alliance_Isra%C3%A9lite_Universelle "Alliance Israélite Universelle"), but it closed in 1923 during the secularization program. Jewish children then attended Turkish schools for a modern education.[[34]](#cite_note-:03222-38)

As of 2021, there are 60 Jews left in Bursa, one active synagogue and one Jewish cemetery.[[35]](#cite_note-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUR_present_with_provinces.svg/4000px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUR_present_with_provinces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUR_present_with_provinces.svg "Köppen map of Bursa Province and surrounding regions:[37]   BSk   Csa   Csb   Cfa   Dsb   Dsc")

The area covered by Bursa corresponds to 1.41% of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")'s land area, which makes the city 27th in the country in terms of land area.[[38]](#cite_note-42) Bursa stands on the northwestern slopes of [Mount Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ") (known as the [Mysian Olympus](/wiki/Mysian_Olympus "Mysian Olympus") in classical antiquity), on the banks of the [Nilüfer River](/wiki/Nil%C3%BCfer_River "Nilüfer River"), in the southern [Marmara Region](/wiki/Marmara_Region "Marmara Region"). It is the capital city of [Bursa Province](/wiki/Bursa_Province "Bursa Province"), which borders the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") and [Yalova](/wiki/Yalova "Yalova") to the north; [Kocaeli](/wiki/%C4%B0zmit "İzmit") and [Sakarya](/wiki/Sakarya_Province "Sakarya Province") to the northeast; [Bilecik](/wiki/Bilecik "Bilecik") to the east; and [Kütahya](/wiki/K%C3%BCtahya "Kütahya") and [Balıkesir](/wiki/Bal%C4%B1kesir "Balıkesir") to the south.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

Bursa has a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") (*Csa/Cs*) under the [Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") and [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") classifications. The city has hot, dry summers that last from June until September. Winters are cool and damp, also containing the most rainfall. There can be snow on the ground which will last for a week or two. [Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution_in_Turkey "Air pollution in Turkey") is a chronic problem in Bursa.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Bursa_image.jpg/250px-Bursa_image.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bursa_image.jpg)

Bursa is one of the leading industrial and agricultural production centres in Turkey.

Bursa is the largest production centre of the [Turkish automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Turkey "Automotive industry in Turkey").[[44]](#cite_note-48) Factories of [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") producers like [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat"), [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault") and [Karsan](/wiki/Karsan "Karsan"), as well as [automotive](/wiki/Automotive "Automotive") parts producers like [Bosch](/wiki/Robert_Bosch_GmbH "Robert Bosch GmbH"), [Mako](/wiki/Magneti_Marelli "Magneti Marelli"), [Valeo](/wiki/Valeo "Valeo"), [Johnson Controls](/wiki/Johnson_Controls "Johnson Controls"), [Delphi](/wiki/Delphi_Automotive "Delphi Automotive") have been active in the city for decades. The [textile](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry") and [food](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry") industries are equally strong, with [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), [Pepsi Cola](/wiki/Pepsi_Cola "Pepsi Cola") and other beverage brands, as well as fresh and [canned food](/wiki/Canned_food "Canned food") industries being present in the city's organized industrial zones.

Apart from its large automotive industry, Bursa also produces a substantial amount of dairy products (by [Sütaş](/wiki/S%C3%BCta%C5%9F "Sütaş")),[[45]](#cite_note-49) processed food (by *Tat*),[[46]](#cite_note-50) and beverages (by *Uludağ*).[[47]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Hudavendigar_Park_in_Bursa_Turkey.jpg/250px-Hudavendigar_Park_in_Bursa_Turkey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hudavendigar_Park_in_Bursa_Turkey.jpg)

[Nilüfer River](/wiki/Nil%C3%BCfer_River "Nilüfer River") and Hüdavendigar Park

Traditionally, Bursa was famous for being the largest centre of [silk](/wiki/Silk "Silk") trade in the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and later the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") empires, during the period of the lucrative [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"). The city is still a major centre for textiles in Turkey and is home to the Bursa International Textiles and Trade Centre (*Bursa Uluslararası Tekstil ve Ticaret Merkezi*, or *BUTTIM*). Bursa was also known for its fertile soil and agricultural activities, which have decreased in the recent decades due to the heavy industrialization of the city.

Bursa is a major centre for tourism. One of the most popular [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") resorts in Turkey is located on [Mount Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ"), just next to the city proper. Bursa's thermal baths have been used for therapeutical purposes since [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") times. Apart from the baths that are operated by hotels, [Uludağ University](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F_University "Uludağ University") has a [physical therapy](/wiki/Physical_therapy "Physical therapy") centre which also makes use of thermal water.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/J33_718_Stra%C3%9Fenbahngelenkwagen_Silkworm.jpg/250px-J33_718_Stra%C3%9Fenbahngelenkwagen_Silkworm.jpg)](/wiki/File:J33_718_Stra%C3%9Fenbahngelenkwagen_Silkworm.jpg)

Tram type "Silkworm" is produced in Bursa by Turkish manufacturer Durmazlar.

Bursa has a [metro](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") ([Bursaray](/wiki/Bursaray "Bursaray")), [trams](/wiki/Trams_in_Bursa "Trams in Bursa")[[48]](#cite_note-52) and a bus system for inner-city public transport, while taxi cabs are also available. Bursa's [Yenişehir Airport](/wiki/Yeni%C5%9Fehir_Airport "Yenişehir Airport") is 49 km (30 mi) away from the city centre. The citizens of Bursa also prefer Istanbul's airports such as [Istanbul Airport](/wiki/Istanbul_Airport "Istanbul Airport") and [Sabiha Gökçen International Airport](/wiki/Sabiha_G%C3%B6k%C3%A7en_International_Airport "Sabiha Gökçen International Airport") for flights to foreign countries, due to Istanbul's proximity to Bursa. There are numerous daily bus and ferry services between the two cities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Teleferik%2C_Uluda%C4%9F.jpg/250px-Teleferik%2C_Uluda%C4%9F.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teleferik,_Uluda%C4%9F.jpg)

[Bursa – Mt. Uludağ](/wiki/Bursa_Uluda%C4%9F_Gondola "Bursa Uludağ Gondola") [gondola lift](/wiki/Gondola_lift "Gondola lift")

The 8.8 km (5.5 mi) long [Bursa Uludağ Gondola](/wiki/Bursa_Uluda%C4%9F_Gondola "Bursa Uludağ Gondola") ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Teleferik*) connects Bursa with the ski resort areas 1,870 m (6,140 ft) high on the mountain [Uludağ](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F "Uludağ").[[49]](#cite_note-53)

The only railway station in Bursa is the [Harmancık](/wiki/Harmanc%C4%B1k "Harmancık") station on the [Balıkesir](/wiki/Bal%C4%B1kesir "Balıkesir")-[Kütahya](/wiki/K%C3%BCtahya "Kütahya") railway, which was opened in 1930.

The average amount of time people spend commuting with public transit in Bursa, for example to and from work, on a weekday is 62 min. 12% of public transit riders ride for more than 2 hours every day. The average amount of time people wait at a stop or station for public transit is 18 min, while 31% of riders wait for over 20 minutes on average every day. The average distance people usually ride in a single trip on public transit is 8.1 km (5.0 mi), while 17% travel for over 12 km (7.5 mi) in a single direction.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/BursaTeknik%C3%9Cniversitesi_MimarSinanYerle%C5%9Fkesi.jpg/250px-BursaTeknik%C3%9Cniversitesi_MimarSinanYerle%C5%9Fkesi.jpg)](/wiki/File:BursaTeknik%C3%9Cniversitesi_MimarSinanYerle%C5%9Fkesi.jpg)

Bursa Technical University campus

Bursa has two public universities and one private university. [Uludağ University](/wiki/Uluda%C4%9F_University "Uludağ University"), founded in 1975, is the oldest institution of higher education in the city. Founded first as the Bursa University then renamed Uludağ University in 1982,[[51]](#cite_note-55) the university has a student body of 47,000, one of the largest in Turkey. [Bursa Technical University](/wiki/Bursa_Technical_University "Bursa Technical University")[[52]](#cite_note-56) is the second public university of Bursa and was established in 2010, beginning education in the 2011–2012 academic year.

The first private university in Bursa was the [Bursa Orhangazi University](/wiki/Bursa_Orhangazi_University "Bursa Orhangazi University"),[[53]](#cite_note-57) which started education in the 2012–2013 academic year. However, Orhangazi University was shut down by the Turkish government after the failed coup attempt of July 2016.

[Istanbul Commerce University](/wiki/Istanbul_Commerce_University "Istanbul Commerce University") has opened graduate programs in Bursa in 2013.[[54]](#cite_note-58)

The vocational high schools, [Bursa Sports High School](/wiki/Bursa_Sports_High_School "Bursa Sports High School"),[[55]](#cite_note-bb1-59) and [Bursa Agriculture Vocational High School](/wiki/Bursa_Agriculture_Vocational_High_School "Bursa Agriculture Vocational High School"),[[56]](#cite_note-tkp1-60) are located in [Osmangazi](/wiki/Osmangazi "Osmangazi") district.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bursa_B%C3%BCy%C3%BCk%C5%9Fehir_Belediye_Stadium_20181026_%282%29.jpg/250px-Bursa_B%C3%BCy%C3%BCk%C5%9Fehir_Belediye_Stadium_20181026_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bursa_B%C3%BCy%C3%BCk%C5%9Fehir_Belediye_Stadium_20181026_(2).jpg)

[Timsah Arena](/wiki/Timsah_Arena "Timsah Arena") is the home of Bursaspor, which won the [Süper Lig](/wiki/S%C3%BCper_Lig "Süper Lig") (Super League) championship title at the end of the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_S%C3%BCper_Lig "2009–10 Süper Lig").

The city has one professional football club, [Bursaspor](/wiki/Bursaspor "Bursaspor"), which formerly competed in the [Süper Lig](/wiki/S%C3%BCper_Lig "Süper Lig") (Super League), the top-tier of Turkish football, until [finishing 16th](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_S%C3%BCper_Lig#League_table "2018–19 Süper Lig") at the end of the [2018–19 Süper Lig](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_S%C3%BCper_Lig "2018–19 Süper Lig") season and being relegated to the [TFF First League](/wiki/TFF_First_League "TFF First League"). A few years earlier, Bursaspor had managed to become the [Turkish champions](/wiki/List_of_Turkish_football_champions#Performance_since_1957 "List of Turkish football champions") at the end of the [2009–10 Süper Lig](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_S%C3%BCper_Lig "2009–10 Süper Lig") season, thereby becoming the second Anatolian club to ever win the Süper Lig championship title after Trabzonspor. Henceforth, Bursaspor was often considered to be one of the five biggest football clubs in Turkey, along with [Galatasaray](/wiki/Galatasaray "Galatasaray"), Fenerbahçe, Beşiktaş and Trabzonspor. The club's relegation to the TFF First League at the end of the 2018–19 season was a major shock for its fans and became a first in the history of Turkish football. Never had a club which had won the Süper Lig championship title been relegated.

Bursaspor plays its home games at the [Timsah Arena](/wiki/Timsah_Arena "Timsah Arena") (meaning "Crocodile Arena", [crocodile](/wiki/Crocodile "Crocodile") being the mascot of the team), which has a seating capacity of 45,000.

The city has three professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") teams in the [Turkish Basketball League](/wiki/Turkish_Basketball_League "Turkish Basketball League"), Bursaspor and [Tofaş S.K.](/wiki/Tofa%C5%9F_S.K. "Tofaş S.K."), which is among the most successful teams. The club plays its games at the [Tofaş Nilüfer Sports Hall](/wiki/Tofa%C5%9F_Nil%C3%BCfer_Spor_Salonu "Tofaş Nilüfer Spor Salonu"). Also, [Final Spor](/wiki/Final_Spor "Final Spor") plays in the second division.

## Culture and Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Culture and Tourism")]

### Ulu Cami (Grand Mosque)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bursa&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ulu Cami (Grand Mosque)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Ulucami-bursa_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Ulucami-bursa_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ulucami-bursa_-_panoramio.jpg)

The [Grand Mosque](/wiki/Grand_Mosque_of_Bursa "Grand Mosque of Bursa") and Orhan Gazi Square in Bursa

[Ulu Cami](/wiki/Bursa_Grand_Mosque "Bursa Grand Mosque") is the largest mosque in Bursa and a landmark of early [Ottoman architecture](/wiki/Ottoman_architecture "Ottoman architecture"), which incorporated many elements from [Seljuk](/wiki/Anatolian_Seljuk_Sultanate "Anatolian Seljuk Sultanate") architecture.

Ordered by Sultan [Bayezid I](/wiki/Bayezid_I "Bayezid I"), the mosque was designed and built by architect Ali Neccar in 1396–1400. It is a large and rectangular building, with a total of twenty domes that are arranged in four rows of five, and are supported by 12 columns. Supposedly the twenty domes were built instead of the twenty separate mosques which Sultan [Bayezid I](/wiki/Bayezid_I "Bayezid I") had promised for winning the [Battle of Nicopolis](/wiki/Battle_of_Nicopolis "Battle of Nicopolis") in 1396. The mosque has two [minarets](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Bursa_Atat%C3%BCrk_M%C3%BCzesi.JPG/250px-Bursa_Atat%C3%BCrk_M%C3%BCzesi.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bursa_Atat%C3%BCrk_M%C3%BCzesi.JPG)

[Bursa Atatürk Museum](/wiki/Bursa_Atat%C3%BCrk_Museum "Bursa Atatürk Museum")

Inside the mosque, there are 192 monumental wall inscriptions written by the famous [calligraphers](/wiki/Islamic_calligraphy "Islamic calligraphy") of that period. There is also a fountain ([şadırvan](/wiki/%C5%9Ead%C4%B1rvan "Şadırvan")) where worshipers can perform [ritual ablutions](/wiki/Wudu "Wudu") before prayer; the dome over the şadırvan is capped by a skylight which creates a soft, serene light below; thus playing an important role in the illumination of the large building.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Bursa_Ye%C5%9Fil_Camii_-_Green_Mosque_%2825%29.jpg/250px-Bursa_Ye%C5%9Fil_Camii_-_Green_Mosque_%2825%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bursa_Ye%C5%9Fil_Camii_-_Green_Mosque_(25).jpg)

[Yeşil Mosque](/wiki/Ye%C5%9Fil_Mosque "Yeşil Mosque")

The horizontally spacious and dimly lit interior is designed to feel peaceful and contemplative. The subdivisions of space formed by multiple domes and pillars create a sense of privacy and even intimacy. This atmosphere contrasts with the later Ottoman mosques (see for example the works of [Suleiman the Magnificent](/wiki/Suleiman_the_Magnificent "Suleiman the Magnificent")'s chief architect, [Mimar Sinan](/wiki/Mimar_Sinan "Mimar Sinan").) The mosques that were built after the [conquest of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") ([Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul")) by the Ottoman Turks in 1453, and influenced by the design of the 6th century Byzantine basilica of [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia"), had increasingly elevated and large central domes, which create a vertical emphasis that is intended to be more overwhelming; to convey the divine power of Allah, the majesty of the Ottoman Sultan, and the governmental authority of the Ottoman State.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Botanik_park_-Bursa_.09_march_09_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Botanik_park_-Bursa_.09_march_09_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Botanik_park_-Bursa_.09_march_09_-_panoramio.jpg)

Botanical Park of Bursa

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Cumal%C4%B1k%C4%B1z%C4%B1k-01.jpg/250px-Cumal%C4%B1k%C4%B1z%C4%B1k-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cumal%C4%B1k%C4%B1z%C4%B1k-01.jpg)

The village of [Cumalıkızık](/wiki/Cumal%C4%B1k%C4%B1z%C4%B1k "Cumalıkızık"), near Bursa, is a [UNESCO](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") World Heritage Site with [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") era historic houses.
